explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **English-language phrases denoting membership in groups, participation in events, or specification of ages** (such as “members of Congress,” “events of Jan. 6,” or “at the age of 12”).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across diverse English-language contexts: political/legal reports (“members of the Zimbabwe National Army”), healthcare and social services (“members of ethnic minorities”), entertainment narratives (“events of Avengers: Age of Ultron”), and demographic references (“between the ages of 5 and 11”). It also processes institutional, professional, or community groupings (e.g., “members of the media,” “party members,” “family members”).

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: High-activation texts consistently contain explicit membership/event/age constructions in English, while low-activation examples are either in other languages (Tamil, Spanish, Romanian, Vietnamese, etc.) or, when in English, lack these specific phrasal patterns (e.g., news snippets without “members of” or “events of” phrasing).

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects a syntactic-semantic template in English text that highlights collective entities, notable occurrences, or life-stage markers, serving as a linguistic indicator of group affiliation, historical reference, or demographic detail.

results.actual_text: eliminated lifetime caps on coverage, and included children on parents’ plans through the age of 26, it also added massive bureaucracy, spurred massive consolidation, and failed
results.plain_text: Though healthcare has been a primary talking point of the 2020 presidential election, the reality is that neither candidate is today poised to produce the reform consumers most need.  Both Donald Trump and Joe Biden agree that healthcare costs are a problem that must be mitigated. Their understanding that this is a top priority for voters is spot on. What misses the mark are the areas they’re targeting to do so.  One area of focus has been on healthcare insurance coverage. The Trump Administration has tried repeatedly to lessen the government’s role in people’s healthcare coverage options by repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act. Biden, on the other hand, wants to build upon President Obama’s legacy by creating a public option that individuals could choose to buy into.  Neither of these are sound solutions. President Trump has yet to officially introduce a health plan that would replace the ACA. And on the ACA itself, I’ve long been a critic who believes it is a flawed piece of legislation that only made inadequacies in the system worse. While it enabled coverage for preexisting conditions, eliminated lifetime caps on coverage, and included children on parents’ plans through the age of 26, it also added massive bureaucracy, spurred massive consolidation, and failed to move the industry in any meaningful way to the market-based model of the future.  A market-based model is built on transparency in cost and quality, accountability for care delivery across the continuum, and enables real competition for consumers/employers. But instead of understanding the fundamental flaws in the current system and then creating market-based policies to fix them, another place both candidates have focused is on just one part of the industry — pharma.  To that end, the President has voiced support for an international pricing index, which would allow Medicare to set prices for certain medications according to what our European counterparts pay their manufacturers. A pro is that this would put pressure on U.S. manufacturers to reduce costs. But a very serious con is that this could cause manufacturers to preserve their margins by cutting back on R&D. There are underlying complexities here as well — the role of rebates, negotiations with payers regarding formulary status and reimbursement levels, as well as other subsidies which don’t allow for meaningful comparisons.  At the same time, we are not only seeing the awesome power of pharmaceutical innovation in the current race for a Covid-19 vaccine, but also just how much it costs to develop these life-saving medicines. Even with focused effort, drug development is an uncertain science with inherent risks. Without legitimate reward, there will be unwillingness to invest capital to support continued innovation. We should look for balance, not cuts.  Rather than create an international index, Biden’s solution to reduce drug costs is to do away with the legislation that currently keeps CMS from negotiating directly with the manufacturers. He has also said he’d establish a commission responsible for determining the true value of whatever drugs are on the market, in terms of their cost and quality. Here, too, there is complexity and a wide range of political agendas. Too often, the consumer gets lost in the mix.  Pharma, as much as it needs to change, only accounts for a relatively small part of the healthcare dollar — approximately 11-15%. And like the entire healthcare ecosystem, pharma needs to come to terms with outmoded approaches and escalating prices that lack appropriate transparency and impact.  Therefore, healthcare costs and quality is what both politicians would be wise to focus on — not narrowly on issues related to coverage, nor those related to prescription drug prices. Both are symptoms of a broader set of issues and distract from solving the underlying problem, which is that the entire healthcare model is fundamentally broken. All of the components — healthcare delivery, pharma, medical device, payers (traditional insurers and employers) and consumers, need to embrace a different model. Consumers need to be front and center, need providers to utilize available technology to enable care to be accessible in ways never before possible, and they need to be engaged in managing their own healthcare in a market-based model that allows them to make decisions as they do in other parts of their lives.  We cannot fix healthcare by arguing at the margins. We’ve been doing that unsuccessfully for over 30 years. Payment needs to be connected to outcomes that matter. Insurance needs to do what it was intended to do. Innovation is there to enable consumers, physicians, and other healthcare providers to engage differently. Too many players, including well-intended politicians, are wedded to the interests of the status quo, playing one off against the other.  What consumers really need leadership to do is develop a more strategic and comprehensive approach for healthcare; a plan that involves realigning incentives and creating a fundamentally different model built on a foundation of transparency (in cost and quality) and accountability (in tying payment to outcomes across the continuum).  The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) recognizes the need for a different approach. During the country’s fight against Covid-19, CMS did away with the bureaucracy that was unnecessarily keeping consumers from accessing safer, more convenient care. They expanded coverage for telehealth services, and also allowed physicians to practice across state lines.  If their goal is to truly make American healthcare more accessible and affordable, the next President must make mandatory the pillars of a market-based model: transparency, accountability, and competition to produce outcomes that matter.  In such models, providers would find reason to embrace telehealth — a tool that keeps patients in the comfort of their home unless an in-person visit is absolutely necessary; that supports the reality that hospitals were never meant to be destinations of choice; and that really is here to stay. They would also find reason to finally heed the 250-year-old wisdom that “an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure,” and ultimately invest in the social determinants of health that have been long neglected.  A market-based healthcare system like that described above is what Americans want. It’s what Americans will choose, and it’s what Americans need. And, perhaps most importantly, it’s what Americans will expect their next leader to establish.

results.plain_text: HONG KONG, Sept. 1 -- The Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR) government has severely condemned the illegal and violent acts of the radical protesters, which continued to escalate with no regard for the safety of the public.  Noting the issuance of a notice of objection by the police, a spokesperson of the HKSAR government said in a statement issued late Saturday that unauthorized assembly and procession in various districts on Hong Kong Island and in Kowloon on Saturday extensively blocked thoroughfares and paralyzed traffic on Hong Kong Island, seriously affecting emergency services and people's daily living.  The radical protesters confronted police in various areas, hurled numerous petrol bombs at the government buildings, the Legislative Council building and the Police Headquarters as well as in other places, and attacked police officers with corrosive liquid and bricks, said the statement.  The radical protesters set fires in different places, vandalized public property, set up barricades, and damaged facilities at MTR stations such as platform screen doors, said the statement, adding that the fire at Hennessy Road was very fierce at one point, posing serious danger.  The behaviors of the radical protesters gravely breached the public peace and posed a serious threat to the safety of police officers on duty and members of the public at the scene, it said.  Stressing that the police will strictly follow up on all the illegal and violent acts, the statement said that the HKSAR government appealed once again to members of the public to say no to violence together, so that order can be restored in society as soon as possible.

results.actual_text: Humanities Elaine Treharne framed the message as an infringement on professors’ freedom of speech, a characterization that University and English department representatives disputed Sunday. Trehar
results.plain_text: English faculty told to redirect press questions on sexual assault allegations to University communications November 13, 2017 0 Comments Share tweet Brian Contreras By: Brian Contreras After two public accusations of sexual assault were made against former Stanford English professors last week, an email from the English department chair told faculty to redirect press questions to the University rather than talk to the media themselves. (KEVIN HSU/The Stanford Daily). Roberta Bowman Denning Professor of Humanities Elaine Treharne framed the message as an infringement on professors’ freedom of speech, a characterization that University and English department representatives disputed Sunday. Treharne criticized the message in light of the administration’s vocal support of free speech ahead of self-proclaimed Islamophobe Robert Spencer’s Nov. 14 visit to campus. “I find it hilarious (not hilarious) that on the day the Provost ‘defends free speech,’ a mandate comes down from University Communications that no English Department faculty member is permitted to speak to the press,” wrote Treharne in a Friday post to a public Facebook group. Treharne was referring to remarks on free speech Provost Persis Drell made to the Faculty Senate on Nov. 9. “We need to defend free speech,” Drell said at the time. “If it costs, it will cost.” According to University spokesman E.J. Miranda, the request was intended to protect involved parties’ confidentiality rather than limit employees’ expression. “There is no restriction on any member of the university community – faculty and students are welcome to share their personal perspectives on any issue with media, or anyone else,” wrote Miranda. “However, faculty members and university employees are bound by the laws mentioned above, which means they must honor confidentiality and privacy laws when they do engage with media as representatives of the university.” Alex Woloch, English department chair and Richard W. Lyman Professor of the Humanities, also wrote that privacy concerns were the main motivator behind the message in an email to The Daily Sunday evening. “My understanding of this is that because the University’s investigations of these cases are bound by strict confidentiality rules, and in order to protect the integrity of possible investigations, they want to make sure that any statements about these cases from representatives of the University are as accurate as possible, and that they do not violate any confidentiality or privacy regulations,” he wrote. But “all faculty should feel free to speak their minds about these important and pressing issues,” Woloch said. Information about the ultimate source of the instruction to faculty has conflicted. While Treharne and Woloch attributed the message to University Communications, Vice President for University Communications Lisa Lapin said Woloch sent out the message in conjunction with the School of Humanities and Sciences communications office. “University Communications did not provide this instruction,” Lapin wrote to the Daily. “It was the [H&S] school communications person working with the department chair.” Lapin acknowledged issues with the message given to faculty, saying Woloch’s note was “not correct” because it did not explain the confidentiality laws that keep the University from discussing the cases in question. In the meantime, English faculty members The Daily contacted about the sexual harassment and assault allegations have given mixed responses. While some professors declined to comment altogether, David Palumbo-Liu, Louise Hewlett Nixon Professor and Professor, by courtesy, of English, offered a statement on the issue. “I am not interested in talking about these professors’ work and roles at Stanford,” Palumbo-Liu wrote in an email to the Daily. “What is of more concern for me is the violence they perpetrated upon extremely vulnerable individuals, individuals who trusted them with their graduate careers and whose trust they betrayed in the worst way possible.” Treharne did not comment for this article, and had changed the privacy setting on the group she posted in to private as of Sunday night. Contact Brian Contreras at brianc42 ‘at’ stanford.edu. 2017-11-13 Brian Contreras November 13, 2017 0 Comments Share tweet Subscribe Click here to subscribe to our daily newsletter of top headlines.

results.plain_text: MILANO - L'Ance, Associazione nazionale dei costruttori edili, chiama a raccolta 19 sigle del comparto per chiedere una volta per tutte proroga e semplificazioni sul Superbonus edilizio al 110% per i lavori di efficienza energetica e anti-sismici. Una misura "di sviluppo sostenibile e di miglioramento ambientale", ha spiegato il presidente Gabriele Buia durante l'evento online, per il rilancio rapido e la crescita dell'occupazione in un comparto che vale il 22% del Pil in cui 1 miliardo investito ne porta 3,5 di benefici e 15.500

results.plain_text: Sinteza zilei. Document exclusiv în scandalul protocoalelor secrete  Mihai Gâdea a prezentat joi seară la „Sinteza zilei” un document exclusiv în scandalul protocoalelor.  Nicolae Popa nu mai era președinte al ÎCCJ la momentul semnării protocolului secret SRI-ÎCCJ.  Iată răspunsul Consiliului Superior al Magistraturii:  Fostul preşedinte al Înaltei Curţi de Casaţie şi Justiţie Nicolae Popa, a cărui semnătură apare pe protocolul de cooperare între SRI, Parchetul General şi Înalta Curte, a declarat la Antena 3 că va face tot ce depinde de el pentru a afla cum este posibil ca semnătura lui să apară pe document, în 2 septembrie 2009, în condiţiile în care el cu o zi înainte, pe 1 septembrie 2009, a depus la CSM cerere de pensionare şi a plecat definitiv din ÎCCJ.
